critical_patterns.title: Full System Deletion
critical_patterns.message: This command will recursively and forcefully delete ALL files starting from the root directory. This will destroy your entire operating system and all data. There is NO recovery without a full system backup.

critical_patterns.title: Full System Deletion
critical_patterns.message: This command will recursively and forcefully delete ALL files starting from the root directory. This will destroy your entire operating system and all data. There is NO recovery without a full system backup.

critical_patterns.title: Root Filesystem Destruction
critical_patterns.message: Deletes the entire root filesystem. This is one of the most dangerous commands in Linux. Your system will be completely destroyed.

critical_patterns.title: Root Filesystem Destruction
critical_patterns.message: Deletes the entire root filesystem. Your system will be completely destroyed.

critical_patterns.title: Fork Bomb Detected
critical_patterns.message: This is a FORK BOMB. It creates an exponentially growing number of processes that will completely freeze and crash your system. Do NOT execute this.

critical_patterns.title: Disk Overwrite
critical_patterns.message: This command will overwrite your physical disk with zeros or random data. ALL DATA ON THAT DISK WILL BE PERMANENTLY DESTROYED. This is irreversible.

critical_patterns.title: Direct Disk Write
critical_patterns.message: Writing directly to a disk device. This can destroy partition tables, filesystems, and all data on the device.

critical_patterns.title: Filesystem Format
critical_patterns.message: mkfs creates a new filesystem on a device, erasing ALL existing data on that partition or disk. Ensure you are targeting the correct device.

critical_patterns.title: Disk Format
critical_patterns.message: This will format a disk or partition, permanently destroying all data on it.

critical_patterns.title: Recursive 777 on Root
critical_patterns.message: Setting 777 permissions recursively on the root filesystem removes all security restrictions. Any user or process can read, write, or execute any file. This is a severe security vulnerability.

critical_patterns.title: Full Permissions on Root
critical_patterns.message: Setting 777 on the root directory removes access controls. This is a critical security risk.

critical_patterns.title: Moving Files to /dev/null
critical_patterns.message: Moving files to /dev/null permanently destroys them. Unlike deletion, there is no way to recover files sent to /dev/null.

critical_patterns.title: Disk Overwrite via Cat
critical_patterns.message: Piping /dev/zero or random data to a disk device will destroy all data on that device permanently.

critical_patterns.title: Redirect to Disk Device
critical_patterns.message: Redirecting output to a disk device will overwrite the disk's data, potentially destroying the filesystem.

critical_patterns.title: Listing Disk Partitions
critical_patterns.message: fdisk -l is read-only and safe for listing partitions. No data will be modified.

critical_patterns.title: Partition Table Editor
critical_patterns.message: fdisk can modify disk partition tables. Incorrect usage can make your data inaccessible. Make sure you know the correct device before proceeding.

critical_patterns.title: Partition Manipulation
critical_patterns.message: parted can resize, delete, and create disk partitions. Mistakes can lead to data loss. Always backup important data first.

high_patterns.title: Recursive File Deletion
high_patterns.message: This will recursively delete files and directories. Deleted files cannot be recovered from the Recycle Bin — they are permanently removed. Double-check the target path before executing.

high_patterns.title: Forced File Deletion
high_patterns.message: The -f flag suppresses all confirmation prompts. Files will be deleted without asking for confirmation.

high_patterns.title: System Shutdown
high_patterns.message: This will shut down the system. Save all open work before executing. A scheduled shutdown can be cancelled with 'shutdown -c'.

high_patterns.title: System Reboot
high_patterns.message: This will reboot the system. All unsaved work will be lost. Make sure to save all open files first.

high_patterns.title: System Halt
high_patterns.message: This will halt the system, stopping all processes. Ensure all work is saved before running.

high_patterns.title: System Power Off
high_patterns.message: This will power off the machine immediately. Save all open work first.

high_patterns.title: Force Kill Process
high_patterns.message: SIGKILL (-9) forcefully terminates a process without allowing it to clean up. The process cannot save state, close files, or release resources gracefully. Use SIGTERM first when possible.

high_patterns.title: Kill All Matching Processes
high_patterns.message: killall sends a signal to ALL processes matching the specified name. Be careful not to accidentally kill system-critical processes.

high_patterns.title: World-Writable Permissions
high_patterns.message: Permission 777 allows any user on the system to read, write, and execute this file. This is a security risk, especially for scripts, configuration files, and directories.

high_patterns.title: World-Readable/Writable File
high_patterns.message: Permission 666 allows any user to read and write to this file. Avoid this for sensitive files.

high_patterns.title: Changing Ownership to Root
high_patterns.message: Changing file ownership to root can affect system security. Ensure this is intentional.

high_patterns.title: Low-Level Disk Operation
high_patterns.message: dd reads and writes data at a low level and can overwrite disks, partitions, and filesystems. Verify the 'if' (input) and 'of' (output) parameters carefully. A mistake can be catastrophic.

high_patterns.title: Privileged File Deletion
high_patterns.message: Running rm with sudo gives it root privileges, allowing deletion of system files and protected directories. Be very careful about what path you are deleting.

high_patterns.title: Remove All Cron Jobs
high_patterns.message: crontab -r removes ALL scheduled cron jobs for the current user. This is irreversible without a backup.

high_patterns.title: Delete User Account
high_patterns.message: userdel deletes a user account. The -r flag will also remove their home directory and mail spool. This action may be difficult to reverse.

high_patterns.title: Delete Group
high_patterns.message: Deleting a group may affect file permissions for files owned by that group.

high_patterns.title: Flush Firewall Rules
high_patterns.message: Flushing iptables rules removes ALL firewall rules, potentially exposing your system to network threats if applied on a live server.

high_patterns.title: Disable Firewall
high_patterns.message: Disabling the firewall removes all network traffic restrictions. Only do this in a trusted environment.

high_patterns.title: Disable SELinux Enforcement
high_patterns.message: Setting SELinux to permissive mode disables mandatory access controls, reducing system security.

high_patterns.title: Enable IP Forwarding
high_patterns.message: Enabling IP forwarding allows the system to route network packets. This can be a security concern if not intentional.

medium_patterns.title: File Deletion
medium_patterns.message: rm permanently deletes files. Unlike moving to Trash, deleted files cannot easily be recovered. Always verify the target path.

medium_patterns.title: Move / Rename Files
medium_patterns.message: mv moves or renames files. If the destination already exists, it will be silently overwritten unless -i (interactive) is used.

medium_patterns.title: Change File Permissions
medium_patterns.message: Changing file permissions affects who can read, write, or execute the file. Incorrect permissions can cause security issues or break applications.

medium_patterns.title: Change File Ownership
medium_patterns.message: Changing ownership of a file may make it inaccessible to the previous owner or affect application behavior.

medium_patterns.title: Stopping or Disabling Service
medium_patterns.message: Stopping or disabling a service may affect system functionality. Ensure you know what the service does before disabling it.

medium_patterns.title: Enabling Service at Boot
medium_patterns.message: This will configure the service to start automatically on boot. Make sure you want this service running persistently.

medium_patterns.title: Package Removal
medium_patterns.message: Removing packages may uninstall software dependencies needed by other applications. Check what will be removed before confirming.

medium_patterns.title: Package Removal
medium_patterns.message: Removing packages may affect other installed software. Review the list of packages to be removed carefully.

medium_patterns.title: Package Removal (Arch)
medium_patterns.message: Removing packages may affect dependencies. Use -Rns to remove the package, its dependencies, and config files.

medium_patterns.title: Python Package Change
medium_patterns.message: Modifying Python packages may affect virtual environments or other Python applications on the system.

medium_patterns.title: Pipe to Shell (curl | bash)
medium_patterns.message: Downloading and executing scripts directly from the internet is extremely dangerous. The script could contain malicious code. Always review scripts before executing them.

medium_patterns.title: Pipe to Shell (wget | bash)
medium_patterns.message: Executing downloaded scripts directly is a security risk. Review the script content at the URL before executing.

medium_patterns.title: Netcat Shell Execution
medium_patterns.message: The -e flag with netcat can create a reverse shell, often used for remote code execution. This may indicate a security exploit or backdoor.

medium_patterns.title: Sudo Shell Access
medium_patterns.message: sudo -s opens a root shell, giving you unrestricted superuser access. All subsequent commands in this shell run as root.

medium_patterns.title: Switch to Root
medium_patterns.message: su without arguments switches to the root account. Running as root allows unrestricted access to the system.

medium_patterns.title: Changing Password
medium_patterns.message: This will change a user account password. With sudo, you can change any user's password.

medium_patterns.title: Clearing Command History
medium_patterns.message: Clearing command history removes the record of commands run in the shell. This can affect auditability.

medium_patterns.title: File Size Manipulation
medium_patterns.message: truncate or fallocate can resize or zero out files. If pointed at the wrong file, data can be lost.

medium_patterns.title: Unmounting Filesystem
medium_patterns.message: Unmounting a filesystem while it is in use can cause data loss. Ensure no processes are accessing the mount before unmounting.

medium_patterns.title: Filesystem Check
medium_patterns.message: Running fsck on a mounted filesystem can cause data corruption. Always unmount the filesystem or run fsck from a live environment.

medium_patterns.title: System State Change via Systemctl
medium_patterns.message: This will change the power state of the system. Save all work before proceeding.

low_patterns.title: Superuser Privilege Escalation
low_patterns.message: sudo executes the following command with root/superuser privileges. The command will have unrestricted access to the system.

low_patterns.title: Scheduling Cron Job
low_patterns.message: crontab manages scheduled tasks. Incorrect entries can cause unintended periodic actions on your system.

low_patterns.title: Remote Connection
low_patterns.message: SSH connects to a remote host. Ensure you trust the destination host before connecting.

low_patterns.title: Remote File Transfer
low_patterns.message: This transfers files to or from a remote host. Verify the source and destination paths carefully.

low_patterns.title: Setting Environment Variable
low_patterns.message: export sets an environment variable that will be available to child processes. Some variables like PATH can affect system behavior.

low_patterns.title: Package Installation / Upgrade
low_patterns.message: Installing or upgrading packages modifies your system. Only install packages from trusted sources.

low_patterns.title: Package Installation / Upgrade
low_patterns.message: Installing or upgrading packages modifies your system. Ensure packages are from official repositories.

low_patterns.title: Package Installation (Arch)
low_patterns.message: Installing packages with pacman modifies the system. Packages from the AUR should be reviewed before installation.

low_patterns.title: Run Command in Background
low_patterns.message: nohup runs a command that continues after you log out. The process will keep running in the background.

low_patterns.title: Sourcing Script in Current Shell
low_patterns.message: Sourcing a script executes it in the current shell environment. Variables and functions defined in the script will affect your current session.
